title: Use ASP.NET Core Identity with JWT for Authentication
context: System requires authentication for Admins, Carriers, and optionally Passengers. Need stateless auth for mobile app/SPA.
decision: Use standard ASP.NET Core Identity combined with JWT Bearer authentication.
consequences: Standard solution, easy to implement. Tokens need to be managed (refresh tokens) for mobile UX.

title: QuestPDF for Schedule Generation
context: Need to generate high-quality, printable PDF schedules for bus stops. HTML-to-PDF converters can be unreliable with layout.
decision: Use QuestPDF library (C# code-first PDF generation).
consequences: Layout defined in C# code, strong typing, no external browser dependency (like Puppeteer/wkhtmltopdf). Performance is generally better.

title: PostgreSQL with PostGIS for Spatial Data
context: System heavily relies on geospatial data (stops, vehicle positions, route shapes).
consequences: Industry standard for OSS spatial data. Npgsql provides good support. Requires PostGIS installed on DB server.

title: SignalR for Real-Time Updates
context: Passengers need to see vehicle positions and delays in real-time.
decision: Use SignalR for pushing updates to connected clients.
consequences: Requires sticky sessions if scaling out (Redis backplane). fallback to polling for clients that don't support it (though unlikely in modern mobile). MVP can start with simple polling if SignalR is too complex, but SignalR is preferred for UX.

title: Separate Read Model for Schedules (CQRS)
context: Schedule data is read-heavy (passengers) and complex to query (joins). Write model is complex (normalized).
decision: Use a denormalized Read Model (possibly JSONB or flat tables) for public schedule queries.
consequences: Faster reads. Requires synchronization (event handlers) between Write and Read models. Adds complexity but necessary for <1.5s response goal.
